steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Something in `/app` handles the bundled basalt, flint, shale, garnet, mica, and opal inputs on simple layouts. The combined report drifts once reordered block layouts enter the pipeline. Iteration tallies stall, residual columns disagree with independent measurement, or rows stay dirty even though the iterate looks settled.

Restore `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` so it writes `/app/output/solver-audit.json`. The per-case helper `bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` matching the matrix slice for the same name. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.

The JSON has a top-level cases array. Each case reports name, ok, iterations, final_residual, reported_residual, residual_agrees, and hit_limit as documented in `/app/docs/layout.md`. Rebuild with CMake before re-running the audit scripts.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@4cc4b56ff70c:/app#

Analysis: We are at /app with no prior commands shown. The task is to restore the audit output generation for both scripts and ensure deterministic JSON matching between matrix and per-case runs. We need to inspect the repository, docs specification, current scripts, output expectations, build with CMake, run scripts, compare outputs, and then fix code/scripts until /app/output/solver-audit.json and /app/output/<name>-audit.json are produced consistently and deterministically.
Plan: 1) Inspect docs to understand required JSON fields and meaning. 2) Inspect current scripts run-matrix.sh and run-one.sh to see what they do and what they currently output. 3) Locate the solver binary, expected inputs, and any existing audit/report generation code. 4) Rebuild with CMake in a clean build dir. 5) Run run-matrix.sh and confirm /app/output/solver-audit.json is produced; run run-one.sh for a case and confirm matching slice JSON. 6) If missing or unstable, patch scripts (and possibly solver output flags) to emit deterministic JSON (stable ordering, fixed numeric formatting), then rerun twice and diff for equivalence. 7) Validate per-case JSON equals matrix slice for that case.

root@4cc4b56ff70c:/app# sed -n '1,200p' /app/docs/layout.md
# Layout notes

Internal modules under `/app` assemble case data into packed layouts used by the iterative driver.

The matrix audit at `/app/output/solver-audit.json` contains a top-level `cases` array. Each element carries name, ok, iterations, final_residual, reported_resi
dual, residual_agrees, and hit_limit as defined below.

- `name` — scenario token
- `ok` — overall row status
- `iterations` — PCG iteration count
- `final_residual` — measured L2 residual after the solve
- `reported_residual` — value emitted through the metric helper
- `residual_agrees` — whether the two residual fields match within tolerance
- `hit_limit` — whether the iteration cap was reached

Per-case output from `run-one.sh` follows the same shape inside a one-element `cases` array written to `/app/output/<name>-audit.json`.

CMake build trees may live under `/app/build`.
root@4cc4b56ff70c:/app# sed -n '1,200p' /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
APP_ROOT="${APP_ROOT:-/app}"
OUT="${APP_ROOT}/output"
mkdir -p "${OUT}"
bash "${APP_ROOT}/scripts/clean-room.sh"
"${APP_ROOT}/bin/solverctl" matrix
root@4cc4b56ff70c:/app# sed -n '1,200p' /app/scripts/run-one.sh
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
APP_ROOT="${APP_ROOT:-/app}"
NAME="${1:?case name required}"
OUT="${APP_ROOT}/output"
mkdir -p "${OUT}"
bash "${APP_ROOT}/scripts/clean-room.sh"
"${APP_ROOT}/bin/solverctl" one "${NAME}"

steps.message:
Analysis: CMake build succeeded but produced the executable at /app/build/bin/solverctl, while the scripts invoke /app/bin/solverctl, causing “No such file or directory”. As a result no audit JSON is generated. We need to restore scripts so they use the built binary (likely from /app/build/bin/solverctl) and ensure they write the required JSON files (/app/output/solver-audit.json and /app/output/<name>-audit.json). Next step is to inspect CMakeLists/install behavior, confirm binary location(s), inspect solverctl CLI output, and then patch scripts (or build to place binary in /app/bin) to reliably create deterministic JSON outputs.
Plan: 1) Inspect /app/build/bin and /app/bin to confirm where solverctl exists. 2) Inspect CMakeLists.txt for output path or install rules. 3) Run solverctl directly from /app/build/bin to see what `matrix` and `one` commands emit and whether they already write audit files somewhere else. 4) Patch run-matrix.sh and run-one.sh to locate solverctl robustly (prefer /app/build/bin/solverctl if present, else /app/bin/solverctl), and to ensure the expected JSON files are written in /app/output (either by passing an output flag if solverctl supports it, or by capturing stdout to the correct file). 5) Re-run matrix and one, verify files exist and per-case matches matrix slice; run matrix twice and diff to ensure deterministic output.

apply_patch<<'PATCH'
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: scripts/run-matrix.sh
@@
 APP_ROOT="${APP_ROOT:-/app}"
 OUT="${APP_ROOT}/output"
 mkdir -p "${OUT}"
 bash "${APP_ROOT}/scripts/clean-room.sh"
-"${APP_ROOT}/bin/solverctl" matrix
+SOLVERCTL="${APP_ROOT}/bin/solverctl"
+if [[ ! -x "${SOLVERCTL}" && -x "${APP_ROOT}/build/bin/solverctl" ]]; then
+  SOLVERCTL="${APP_ROOT}/build/bin/solverctl"
+fi
+
+"${SOLVERCTL}" matrix >"${OUT}/solver-audit.json"
*** End Patch
PATCH

apply_patch<<'PATCH'

*** Update File: scripts/run-one.sh
@@

-"${APP_ROOT}/bin/solverctl" one "${NAME}"

+  SOLVERCTL="${APP_ROOT}/build/bin/solverctl"
+fi
+
+"${SOLVERCTL}" one "${NAME}" >"${OUT}/${NAME}-audit.json"
*** End Patch
PATCH

python3 - <<'PY'
import json
from pathlib import Path
M=json.loads(Path('/app/output/solver-audit.json').read_text())
O=json.loads(Path('/app/output/basalt-audit.json').read_text())
mc={c['name']:c for c in M['cases']}
oc={c['name']:c for c in O['cases']}
print('matrix has basalt:', 'basalt' in mc)
print('one has basalt   :', 'basalt' in oc)
print('equal:', mc.get('basalt')==oc.get('basalt'))
if mc.get('basalt')!=oc.get('basalt'):
  print('matrix basalt:', mc.get('basalt'))
  print('one basalt  :', oc.get('basalt'))
PY

python3 - <<'PY'
import json
from pathlib import Path
p1=Path('/app/output/solver-audit.run1.json')
p2=Path('/app/output/solver-audit.json')
a=json.loads(p1.read_text())
b=json.loads(p2.read_text())
print('equivalent:', a==b)
print('cases:', len(a.get('cases',[])), len(b.get('cases',[])))
PY

steps.message:
Analysis: The solver binary exists only at /app/build/bin/solverctl; /app/bin does not exist. Running solverctl directly with `matrix` already writes /app/output/solver-audit.json (as seen once), and src/main.cpp references emitting to that fixed path. The scripts are still broken because they call /app/bin/solverctl. My attempted patch failed because `apply_patch` is unavailable in this environment, so the scripts remain unchanged. We need to edit scripts using available tools (e.g., cat/heredoc or sed) to point to /app/build/bin/solverctl (or generally locate it). Also, my earlier redirection idea is wrong because solverctl appears to write its own files; redirecting stdout isn’t required. We should restore scripts to invoke the correct binary and rely on solverctl’s internal emit for audit JSON. Then validate run-one creates /app/output/<name>-audit.json and matches matrix slice, and ensure two matrix runs are equivalent.
Plan: 1) Inspect current scripts to confirm contents. 2) Rewrite run-matrix.sh and run-one.sh using heredoc to: (a) locate solverctl at /app/build/bin/solverctl first (or allow override), (b) run clean-room, (c) run the command without stdout redirection. 3) Ensure scripts are executable. 4) Re-run build (already built, but keep as-is), run matrix and one(basalt), check that expected JSON files exist. 5) Compare per-case basalt JSON to matrix slice. 6) Run matrix twice and compare JSON for exact equality (or at least semantic equality via JSON parse).

arguments.keystrokes:
cat > /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

APP_ROOT="${APP_ROOT:-/app}"
OUT="${APP_ROOT}/output"
mkdir -p "${OUT}"

# Prefer the CMake build tree binary. Allow an override via SOLVERCTL.
SOLVERCTL="${SOLVERCTL:-}"
if [[ -z "${SOLVERCTL}" ]]; then
  if [[ -x "${APP_ROOT}/build/bin/solverctl" ]]; then
    SOLVERCTL="${APP_ROOT}/build/bin/solverctl"
  else
    SOLVERCTL="${APP_ROOT}/bin/solverctl"
  fi
fi

bash "${APP_ROOT}/scripts/clean-room.sh"
"${SOLVERCTL}" matrix
EOF

cat > /app/scripts/run-one.sh <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

APP_ROOT="${APP_ROOT:-/app}"
NAME="${1:?case name required}"
OUT="${APP_ROOT}/output"
mkdir -p "${OUT}"

# Prefer the CMake build tree binary. Allow an override via SOLVERCTL.
SOLVERCTL="${SOLVERCTL:-}"
if [[ -z "${SOLVERCTL}" ]]; then
  if [[ -x "${APP_ROOT}/build/bin/solverctl" ]]; then
    SOLVERCTL="${APP_ROOT}/build/bin/solverctl"
  else
    SOLVERCTL="${APP_ROOT}/bin/solverctl"
  fi
fi

bash "${APP_ROOT}/scripts/clean-room.sh"
"${SOLVERCTL}" one "${NAME}"
EOF

python3 - <<'PY'
import json

steps.message:
Analysis: run-one.sh now works and produces basalt-audit.json, but run-matrix.sh no longer produces solver-audit.json. Earlier, running /app/build/bin/solverctl matrix directly did create solver-audit.json, so something about the new run-matrix path/clean-room or environment is preventing the file from being written (or it is being written elsewhere/then removed). We need to: (1) confirm which solverctl binary is being invoked, (2) inspect clean-room.sh (it may remove solver-audit.json after matrix run or change directories), (3) run solverctl matrix with tracing to see actions, (4) inspect src/main.cpp matrix behavior and current emit path/conditions, and (5) adjust script accordingly (possibly run solverctl matrix from /app as CWD, or set APP_ROOT, or avoid clean-room wiping output after run).
Plan: 1) Add bash -x trace for run-matrix.sh to confirm selected SOLVERCTL and working dir. 2) Inspect clean-room.sh to see if it deletes /app/output/solver-audit.json or manipulates CWD/APP_ROOT. 3) Run solverctl matrix manually again and check whether it creates the file now. 4) Inspect src/main.cpp around matrix path to understand where it writes and any conditions. 5) If clean-room is deleting after run, reorder: run clean-room first (already), but ensure it doesn’t remove output directory after solver runs; possibly recreate /app/output after clean-room, or modify clean-room to not delete audit outputs, or adjust scripts to ensure output directory exists post-clean-room. 6) Validate outputs and determinism after fix.

Analysis: run-matrix.sh is now correctly invoking /app/build/bin/solverctl and clean-room.sh. With tracing, solverctl matrix successfully produced /app/output/solver-audit.json. The earlier missing file was due to our rm step followed by an unverified run; now matrix output exists. Next we must: run run-one.sh for basalt again (clean-room wipes output each time, so ordering matters), then compare per-case JSON with matrix slice. Also verify two matrix runs from unchanged inputs are equivalent JSON. Because clean-room removes output, we should copy solver-audit.json aside before running run-one, or run matrix, copy, then run one, then rerun matrix for determinism checks.
Plan: 1) Run run-matrix.sh to generate solver-audit.json, then copy it to a saved file. 2) Run run-one.sh basalt (this will wipe output) and save basalt-audit.json. 3) Regenerate matrix and compare basalt slice with the saved basalt audit. 4) Run matrix twice and compare JSON equality (semantic and exact). If mismatch, inspect emit ordering/formatting; otherwise task is complete.

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

APP_ROOT="${APP_ROOT:-/app}"
OUT="${APP_ROOT}/output"
mkdir -p "${OUT}"

# Prefer the CMake build tree binary. Allow an override via SOLVERCTL.
SOLVERCTL="${SOLVERCTL:-}"
if [[ -z "${SOLVERCTL}" ]]; then
  if [[ -x "${APP_ROOT}/build/bin/solverctl" ]]; then
    SOLVERCTL="${APP_ROOT}/build/bin/solverctl"
  else
    SOLVERCTL="${APP_ROOT}/bin/solverctl"
  fi
fi

bash "${APP_ROOT}/scripts/clean-room.sh"
"${SOLVERCTL}" matrix

Analysis: The saved copies basalt-audit.run1.json and solver-audit.run2.json were created inside /app/output, but that directory is wiped by clean-room.sh at the start of every run-matrix.sh and run-one.sh. So the subsequent run-matrix.sh deleted basalt-audit.run1.json before the Python comparison; similarly, run-matrix.sh deleted solver-audit.run2.json before the determinism comparison. We need to store snapshots outside /app/output (e.g., /tmp) or modify clean-room.sh not to delete snapshot files. We'll rerun and save snapshots to /tmp, then perform comparisons.
Plan: 1) Create /tmp/audit-snap and ensure it persists across runs. 2) Run matrix, copy solver-audit.json to /tmp. 3) Run one basalt, copy basalt-audit.json to /tmp. 4) Run matrix again and compare basalt slice vs saved per-case audit. 5) Save two matrix outputs to /tmp and compare for equality/normalized equality.
